claimed_contribution: The paper claims to reconcile two literatures by showing that the usual bonus-pack preference depends on whether the food is a virtue or a vice.
It claims to identify and test anticipated guilt and guilt-reducing justification as antecedents of self-rationing in vice-food promotion choice.
It claims practical guidance that promotion-food congruency matters: price discounts are more effective for vice foods, whereas bonus packs are more effective for virtue foods.
context: Consumer food choices in healthy versus unhealthy categories, including products such as chocolates, raisins, cake, fruit salad, muffins, and cookies. Evidence comes from a Starbucks intercept survey and controlled lab and simulated shopping experiments with promotional offers.
design_and_data: After a preliminary Starbucks intercept survey (n=98), the authors run five experiments with students and local participants. Experiment 1 (n=120) uses identical chocolates described as healthy versus tasty to test promotion choice and codes thought protocols for guilt-consistent reasoning; Experiment 2 (n=323) uses a 2 x 3 between-subject design (food type x promotion/control) and manipulates reference quantity to hold offered quantity constant. Experiment 3 (n=109) measures dispositional consumption guilt in a simulated shopping task, and Experiment 4 (n=160) manipulates justification by having either the participant or a friend make the choice, including an indifference option. Experiment 5 (n=199) manipulates guilt via a prior donation task and measures subsequent willingness to buy vice and virtue foods under price discount versus bonus pack.
main_findings: Consumers were more likely to choose bonus packs for virtue foods and price discounts for vice foods, and this pattern persisted when offered quantity was held constant via a reference-quantity manipulation.
Guilt-consistent thoughts mediated the effect, and higher dispositional consumption guilt increased preference for price discounts over bonus packs for vice foods but not for virtue foods.

When justification pressure was reduced (friend chooses) or guilt was reduced through a prior donation task, preference for price discounts on vice foods diminished, while virtue foods continued to favor bonus packs.
one_line_summary: Across five experiments, consumers preferred bonus packs for virtue foods but price discounts for vice foods because price discounts better justified vice purchases by reducing anticipated guilt.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior sales-promotion framing research that reports a general bonus-pack advantage by showing a reversal for vice foods. It also connects and builds on self-rationing work by specifying guilt and justification as mechanisms that shape responses to promotion format in food choices.
research_gap: Prior promotion research largely suggests a general preference for bonus packs over price discounts, without clearly accounting for whether the product is a virtue or a vice food. The paper addresses the unresolved mechanism of how anticipated guilt and justification may alter promotion preference, especially for vice foods.
research_question: How do price discount versus bonus pack promotions affect consumer preference for virtue (healthy) and vice (unhealthy) foods? Do guilt and purchase justification explain why vice foods often show a different promotion preference than the general bonus-pack preference reported in prior work?
